{"step_by_step_thinking": "The question asks whether there is an upper limit on the functional fraction of the human genome. The provided documents discuss the presence and nature of pseudogenes, which are non-functional or degraded versions of functional genes. Multiple documents (e.g., Document [0], [10]) estimate that pseudogenes make up up to one-third of the human genome. However, these documents do not explicitly state or imply an upper limit on the functional fraction of the genome. Instead, they highlight that some pseudogenes may have regulatory roles, suggesting that even non-coding or non-functional elements can have biological significance. This implies that the functional fraction may be dynamic and not strictly bounded by a fixed upper limit. The documents do not provide evidence of a theoretical or empirical upper cap on functionality. Therefore, based on the information, there is no indication of a strict upper limit on the functional fraction of the human genome.", "answer_choice": "B"}
